g.data-narration: Introduction to Selection Statements in Java for university Module 1
Selection statements control program flow based on boolean conditions, enabling dynamic decision-making
g.text: Control structures that execute different code blocks based on boolean conditions.
They enable programs to make decisions and respond dynamically to runtime conditions.
Common types: if, if-else, else-if ladder, switch, ternary operator

g.data-narration: Selection statements are essential for validation, business logic, and responding to user input
g.text: • Programs must respond to user input and varying conditions
• Enable validation logic (password checking, form validation)
• Implement business rules and game logic
• Essential for passing university exams and real-world programming

g.data-narration: The simple if statement executes code only when its boolean condition evaluates to true
g.text: Executes code only when a condition evaluates to true. Condition must be boolean.

g.data-narration: The if-else statement provides two distinct code paths based on whether the condition is true or false
g.text: Provides two distinct code paths - one for true, one for false condition.

g.data-narration: The else-if ladder tests multiple conditions sequentially, executing the first true condition's block
g.text: Tests multiple conditions in sequence. First true condition executes its block.

g.data-narration: The switch statement compares a variable against constant values, requiring break to prevent fall-through
g.text: Multi-way branch comparing a variable against constant values. Requires break to prevent fall-through.

g.data-narration: Java 14+ switch expressions use arrow syntax and can return values without fall-through issues
g.text: Arrow syntax (→) eliminates break statements. Can return values - acts as an expression.

g.data-narration: The ternary operator provides compact conditional expression syntax returning one of two values
g.text: Compact conditional operator. Syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse

g.data-narration: The leap year example demonstrates nested if-else with multiple conditions checking divisibility
The calculator example shows practical switch usage with multiple operators and default case handling
g.text: Shows practical switch usage with different operators (+, -, *, /)

g.data-narration: Avoid common mistakes: missing break statements, non-boolean conditions, wrong string comparison
Key points: if statements need boolean conditions, switch requires break, use equals for strings
Remember these exam points: Java requires boolean conditions, switch needs break, use equals for string comparison
g.text: Remember: Java requires boolean conditions | Switch needs break | Use .equals() for Strings
Practice all example programs - they frequently appear in exams!
